name: Pigeon Pose (Modified)

prerequisites.notes: Modified version — knee must tolerate position

steps: From all fours, slide right knee forward behind right wrist.
Extend left leg straight back. Hips stay square.
Fold forward over front leg as tolerated.
Hold 45s. Breathe deeply. Switch sides.
formCues: ✅ Front shin angle based on flexibility

injuryNotes.lower_back: Fold only as far as back allows. Block under hip if needed.
injuryNotes.knee: LEFT knee — if front-leg position causes pain, use figure-4 stretch instead. Knee severity ≤3.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Use blocks under hands if needed.

purpose: Deep hip external rotation stretch — opens piriformis and deep hip rotators.
whyForYou: Opens tight hips that force your back to compensate for missing hip ROM.
proTip: Block under front hip if it doesn't reach floor — keeps hips level.
source: Yoga therapy, Hip mobility protocols

name: Hip Flexor March (Supine, Banded)

prerequisites.notes: Dead bug mastered for core control

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 12 reps each side with band, back flat

steps: Lie on back, band around feet. Knees at 90°.
Back flat on floor. Brace core.
March one knee toward chest against band resistance.
Return slowly. Alternate sides.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Back must stay flat. If it arches, remove band. Core controls everything.
injuryNotes.knee: Knee stays at 90° — no extension stress.

purpose: Hip flexor strengthening without impingement — banded resistance in safe position.
whyForYou: Strengthens hip flexors in a back-safe position. Weak hip flexors contribute to gait compensations.
proTip: Light band only. This is activation, not max strength.
source: Hip rehabilitation protocols

name: Fire Hydrant

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 12 reps each side with hip stable

steps: All fours position. Hands under shoulders, knees under hips.
Keeping knee bent, lift one knee out to the side (abduction + external rotation).
Hold 1s at top. Lower slowly.
Keep torso completely still — movement from hip only.
formCues: ✅ Lift from hip, not by rotating torso

injuryNotes.lower_back: Low demand — all fours position. Torso stays still.
injuryNotes.knee: Knee stays bent — no extension stress. Pad kneeling knee.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Weight on hands — modify if wrist/shoulder bothers you.

purpose: Hip abduction + external rotation activation — targets glute med in a different plane than clamshells.
whyForYou: Builds glute med from a different angle than clamshells. Important for knee tracking protection.
proTip: Add a mini band above knees for extra resistance once bodyweight is easy.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Glute Activation

name: Lateral Band Walk

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 12 steps each direction with constant tension

steps: Band around ankles (harder) or above knees (easier). Quarter squat.
Step RIGHT — push through LEFT heel. Keep tension.
Step LEFT foot in — DON'T let band go slack.
12 steps right, then 12 left.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Stay in quarter squat — no bending forward.
injuryNotes.knee: Band above knees if ankle position bothers knee. This exercise PROTECTS your knee via glute med.

purpose: Functional glute med strengthening — the #1 muscle for knee tracking and pelvic control.
whyForYou: Weak glute med = knee valgus = knee pain. This directly protects your left knee.
proTip: Place hands on glutes — feel them working with every step.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition, Knee rehab protocols

name: Ankle Alphabet (Seated)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full alphabet both feet without pain

steps: Sit with one leg crossed over the other (or hanging off edge).
Using your FOOT (not whole leg), trace each letter of the alphabet.
Move only at the ankle — not the knee.
Complete A-Z, then switch feet.

injuryNotes.knee: No impact — movement below the knee only.

purpose: Full ankle ROM in all planes — assesses and maintains ankle mobility.
whyForYou: Maintains ankle mobility that supports your squat mechanics and sport performance.
proTip: Do this while watching TV — easy to accumulate volume.
source: Ankle rehabilitation protocols

name: Towel Scrunches
steps: Sit in chair, towel flat on floor under bare foot.
Scrunch towel toward you using only your TOES.
Reset towel. Repeat with other foot.
2 full towel lengths per foot.
formCues: ✅ Only toes move — not whole foot

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Intrinsic foot muscle activation — strengthens the arch from the inside out.
whyForYou: Strong intrinsic foot muscles support your arch and improve proprioception for all standing exercises.
proTip: If you cramp, massage the arch and try shorter sets. Foot muscles fatigue fast.
source: Plantar fasciitis protocols, Foot intrinsic research

name: Eccentric Calf Drop (Step)

prerequisites.notes: Calf raise tolerated first. Alfredson protocol.

steps: Stand on step edge — both feet. Rise onto toes.
Shift weight to ONE foot. Lower that heel SLOWLY below step — 3s.
Use both feet to push back up. Repeat single-leg drops.
3 sets of 15 — straight knee first, then bent knee.
formCues: ✅ Both feet up, one foot down
✅ Straight knee AND bent knee sets

injuryNotes.knee: Bent-knee version increases knee demand. Straight knee version is safer for your knee.

purpose: Alfredson eccentric protocol — gold standard for Achilles tendinopathy rehabilitation.
whyForYou: Builds eccentric calf strength. The slow lowering phase strengthens tendons more than concentric work.
proTip: Mild discomfort during the exercise is acceptable. Sharp pain = stop. Per Alfredson protocol.
source: Alfredson H. Heavy-load eccentric calf muscle training

name: Single-Leg Balance on Unstable Surface
steps: Stand on foam pad, BOSU, or folded towel (unstable surface).
Near a wall for safety. Lift one foot.
Balance 20s. Focus on ankle corrections.
Switch legs.

injuryNotes.knee: Unstable surface increases knee demand. Knee severity ≤3. Use wall support.

purpose: Progressed proprioception training — unstable surface forces ankle stabilizers to work harder.
whyForYou: Builds ankle proprioception for surfing, hiking, and BJJ — all require stability on unstable surfaces.
proTip: Start with a folded towel (least unstable), then foam pad, then BOSU.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Balance Progression

name: Toe Yoga (Big Toe Up, Little Toes Down)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Independent control of big toe vs little toes

steps: Barefoot, seated or standing. Feet flat on floor.
Lift ONLY big toe while keeping little toes DOWN.
Then reverse — big toe DOWN, lift little toes UP.
Alternate 10 times each pattern.
formCues: ✅ Keep foot flat — only toes move

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Foot intrinsic control — independent toe movement improves foot mechanics and arch function.
whyForYou: Better foot control improves your foundation for all standing and balance work.
proTip: Most people can't do this at first. Keep practicing — your brain will figure it out in 1-2 weeks.
source: Foot intrinsic training research, Dr. Emily Splichal

name: Tibialis Anterior Raise (Wall)
steps: Stand with back against wall. Feet 12 inches from wall.
Lift TOES off ground — dorsiflex both feet. Keep heels down.
Hold 1s at top. Lower slowly.
Feel the front of your shins burn.
formCues: ✅ Only toes lift — heels stay on ground

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Tibialis anterior strengthening — prevents shin splints and balances calf dominance.
whyForYou: Balances the front/back of your lower leg. Prevents shin splints during walking and hiking progression.
proTip: If you've ever had shin splints, this is your prevention exercise. 3x20 daily.
source: Shin splint prevention protocols

name: Wrist Extensor Stretch
steps: Extend arm straight, palm facing DOWN.
Use other hand to pull fingers back (toward you) and down.
Feel stretch on top of forearm. Hold 30s.
Switch arms.
formCues: ✅ Feel stretch on TOP of forearm
commonMistakes: ❌ Wrong direction (this is extensors, not flexors)

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Wrist extensor stretch — prevents and treats lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow).
whyForYou: Prevents forearm tightness from gripping during deadlifts and carries.
proTip: Do this after every gripping-heavy workout. 30s each arm.
source: Tennis elbow rehabilitation protocols

name: Wrist Flexor Stretch
steps: Extend arm straight, palm facing UP.
Use other hand to pull fingers back (toward you) and down.
Feel stretch on UNDERSIDE of forearm. Hold 30s.
Switch arms.

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Wrist flexor stretch — prevents and treats medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow).
whyForYou: Prevents medial forearm tightness from gripping and pulling exercises.
proTip: Pair with extensor stretch — always stretch both sides of the forearm.
source: Golfer's elbow rehabilitation protocols

name: Eccentric Wrist Extension (Tyler Twist)
steps: Hold FlexBar (or rolled towel) with affected arm on TOP.
Use unaffected hand to twist bar. Hold twist.
Slowly UN-twist using only the affected hand — 3s eccentric.
Reset. Repeat. The SLOW release is the treatment.
formCues: ✅ Mild discomfort OK — sharp pain = stop
commonMistakes: ❌ Stopping because of mild discomfort (expected initially)

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Keep arms in front — no overhead.

purpose: Tyler twist / eccentric wrist extension — gold standard for lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) rehab.
whyForYou: If grip-intensive exercises cause elbow pain, this is the treatment. Eccentric loading heals tendinopathy.
proTip: Mild discomfort during the exercise is expected and acceptable. Sharp pain = stop.
source: Tyler TF et al. (2006) FlexBar study, Alfredson eccentric principles

name: Median Nerve Glide (Carpal Tunnel)
steps: Start: fist at shoulder, wrist flexed, elbow bent.
Slowly extend elbow, open hand, extend wrist back.
You should feel a GENTLE pull from shoulder to hand.
Return to start. Repeat 10 times each arm. NEVER force.
formCues: ✅ Mild pulling sensation only — no pain

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Full arm position — if shoulder bothers you, reduce arm extension.

purpose: Median nerve mobilization — reduces nerve compression symptoms (carpal tunnel prevention).
whyForYou: Preventive for anyone doing grip-heavy work. Keeps the median nerve gliding freely.
proTip: This is a GLIDE not a STRETCH. The nerve should slide, not be stretched. Pain = too aggressive.
source: Butler D. Mobilisation of the Nervous System, Nerve glide protocols

name: Grip Strengthening (Ball Squeeze)
steps: Hold a soft ball, stress ball, or rolled towel in one hand.
Squeeze firmly. Hold 2s. Release.
Progress to harder objects as strength improves.
Can also do sustained holds (20s) for endurance.
commonMistakes: ❌ Too hard too fast (tennis/golfer's elbow risk)

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: No impact — can be done with arm at side.

purpose: General grip and forearm strengthening — supports all pulling and carrying exercises.
whyForYou: Supports your deadlift and carry grip. Can be done anywhere, anytime.
proTip: Do these while watching TV or on phone calls — easy volume accumulation.
source: Hand therapy protocols, Grip strength research

name: Sciatic Nerve Glide

prerequisites.notes: GENTLE — nerve tissue is delicate. Stop if symptoms increase.

steps: Lie on back. Pull one knee toward chest with hands.
SLOWLY extend the knee — straighten the leg toward ceiling.
As you straighten, FLEX the foot (toes toward you) to add nerve tension.
Return to bent position. Repeat 10 times. GENTLE.
formCues: ✅ SLOW and GENTLE — nerve tissue is fragile
✅ Glide, not stretch — nerve should slide

injuryNotes.lower_back: If you have sciatica, this helps determine if it's disc-related or piriformis. STOP if symptoms increase or peripheralize.
injuryNotes.knee: Knee straightens gently — don't force.

purpose: Sciatic nerve mobilization — reduces nerve compression symptoms and improves nerve gliding.
whyForYou: Helps differentiate disc vs piriformis sciatica. Keeps the sciatic nerve gliding freely along your post-surgical back.
proTip: This is a GLIDE not a STRETCH. Alternating foot position (flex/point) slides the nerve back and forth.
source: Butler D. Mobilisation of the Nervous System

name: Femoral Nerve Glide
steps: Lie on your side (affected side UP). Bottom knee bent.
Grab top ankle behind you. Gently pull heel toward glute.
SLOWLY extend the hip (push knee back) while holding ankle.
Return. Repeat. Feel a gentle pull down the front of the thigh.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Side-lying. Don't arch back during hip extension.
injuryNotes.knee: Requires knee flexion — if left knee can't tolerate, reduce range.

purpose: Femoral nerve mobilization — for anterior thigh numbness/tingling symptoms.
whyForYou: If you ever get anterior thigh symptoms from your back, this nerve glide helps.
proTip: The femoral nerve runs through the hip flexor — tight hip flexors can compress it.
source: Butler D. Mobilisation of the Nervous System

name: Ulnar Nerve Glide
steps: Stand or sit. Arm at side.
Extend wrist back (fingers up). Bend elbow — bring hand toward shoulder.
Then reverse: straighten elbow while flexing wrist.
Alternate positions 10 times. Feel gentle pull along inner elbow.

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Ulnar nerve mobilization — for elbow/ring/pinky finger tingling.
whyForYou: Preventive for anyone doing heavy gripping work. Keeps ulnar nerve sliding freely.
proTip: If you lean on elbows at your desk, the ulnar nerve gets compressed. This counteracts that.
source: Butler D. Mobilisation of the Nervous System

name: Median Nerve Glide (Full Arm)
steps: Stand. Arm at side.
Slowly extend arm out to side, palm up. Extend wrist back.
Tilt head AWAY from extended arm for more tension.
Return. Repeat. Feel gentle pull from neck through arm to fingers.
formCues: ✅ Full arm extension — shoulder to fingertips
commonMistakes: ❌ Holding too long (it's a glide, not a stretch)

injuryNotes.knee: No impact.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Full arm extension — if shoulder limits this, reduce range or use hand-focused version.

purpose: Full arm median nerve mobilization — addresses nerve from cervical spine to fingertips.
whyForYou: Comprehensive nerve health for your upper extremity. Especially useful if you have any hand/forearm symptoms.
proTip: Combine with head tilt for maximum nerve mobilization — but only if tolerated.
source: Butler D. Mobilisation of the Nervous System

name: Posterior Pelvic Tilt (Supine)

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 10 reps with full back contact to floor

steps: Lie on back. Knees bent, feet flat.
Flatten your lower back INTO the floor — eliminate the arch.
Hold 5s. You should feel abs and glutes engage.
Release. Repeat.
commonMistakes: ❌ Pushing with legs instead of tilting pelvis

injuryNotes.lower_back: Williams Step 1. For STENOSIS/FACET — flexion-biased protocol. OPPOSITE of McKenzie extension. Choose based on your condition's directional preference.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Williams Step 1 — posterior pelvic tilt to open the lumbar canal (flexion-biased protocol).
whyForYou: Your primary protocol is McKenzie (extension), but this is available if your condition changes or you develop stenosis symptoms.
proTip: Think of pulling your belly button to your spine while pressing back into floor.
source: Williams PC. Low Back and Neck Pain, Physical therapy protocols

name: Single Knee to Chest
steps: Lie on back. Both knees bent.
Pull ONE knee toward chest. Hands behind the knee.
Hold 30s. Feel gentle stretch in lower back and glute.
Switch legs. Keep opposite foot on floor.
formCues: ✅ Hands behind knee — not on shin

injuryNotes.lower_back: Williams Step 2. Unilateral lumbar flexion. Gentler than double knee. Safe for most back conditions except acute disc herniation.
injuryNotes.knee: Hands behind knee — not on shin. Reduce flexion if left knee is uncomfortable.

purpose: Williams Step 2 — gentle unilateral lumbar flexion stretch.
whyForYou: Gentle back stretch. Can be used alongside or as alternative to McKenzie depending on your symptoms.
proTip: One knee at a time is gentler on the back than both knees together.
source: Williams PC. Low Back and Neck Pain

name: Double Knees to Chest
steps: Lie on back. Pull BOTH knees toward chest.
Hands behind both knees. Gently pull.
Hold 30s. Feel full lumbar flexion stretch.
Rock gently side to side if comfortable.
commonMistakes: ❌ Doing this with acute disc symptoms

injuryNotes.lower_back: Williams Step 3. Full lumbar flexion. More loaded than single knee. Back severity ≤4. Not for acute disc.
injuryNotes.knee: Hands behind knees. Reduce flexion if left knee is uncomfortable.

purpose: Williams Step 3 — full bilateral lumbar flexion for stenosis/facet relief.
whyForYou: Opens the lumbar canal. Use when stenosis symptoms are present (not for disc symptoms).
proTip: Gentle rocking side to side in this position gives a nice SI joint mobilization.
source: Williams PC. Low Back and Neck Pain

name: Partial Curl-Up (Williams)

prerequisites.notes: Flexion stretches tolerated first. Back severity ≤3.

steps: Lie on back. Knees bent, feet flat. Arms crossed on chest.
Flatten back to floor (pelvic tilt). Then curl head/shoulders up.
Rise only until shoulder blades clear floor. Hold 2s.
Lower slowly. NOT a full sit-up.
formCues: ✅ This is NOT a full sit-up

injuryNotes.lower_back: Williams Step 4. LOADED flexion — severity must be ≤3. NOT for disc patients. Only for stenosis/facet. Different from McGill curl-up (which preserves lordosis).
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Williams Step 4 — abdominal strengthening in flexion for stenosis/facet conditions.
whyForYou: Different from your McGill curl-up (which preserves the curve). This deliberately flexes. Use ONLY if directed for stenosis.
proTip: This is NOT a McGill curl-up. Williams curls INTO flexion deliberately. Know which protocol you're following.
source: Williams PC. Low Back and Neck Pain

name: Seated Forward Flexion (Williams)

prerequisites.notes: Partial curl tolerated. Williams final step.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Full seated flexion without symptom increase

steps: Sit in chair. Feet flat.
Slide hands down shins. Fold forward from back.
Go to comfortable stretch. Hold 5s.
Return to sitting. Repeat 6 times.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Williams Step 5 (final). Full seated flexion. Severity ≤3. NOT for disc patients.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact.

purpose: Williams Step 5 — end-range seated lumbar flexion. Final progression in Williams protocol.
whyForYou: Final Williams step. Available if your condition warrants flexion-biased treatment.
proTip: Williams and McKenzie are OPPOSITE protocols. Know which one applies to your current condition.
source: Williams PC. Low Back and Neck Pain

name: Single-Leg Balance Eyes Closed
steps: Stand near wall for safety. Lift one foot.
Close eyes. Balance as long as possible (target 20s).
Focus on ankle corrections — what you FEEL, not what you see.
Open eyes if losing balance. Switch legs.

injuryNotes.knee: Eyes closed removes visual compensation — more ankle/hip demand. Knee severity ≤3.

purpose: Removes visual input — doubles proprioceptive challenge compared to eyes-open.
whyForYou: Eyes closed forces your ankle and hip proprioceptors to work without visual backup — crucial for surfing and BJJ.
proTip: Most falls happen in low-light. Training balance without vision is practical life insurance.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Balance Progression

name: Single-Leg Balance on BOSU

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 20s each leg on BOSU flat side

steps: Stand on BOSU (flat side up) near a rack for safety.
Lift one foot. Find balance.
Hold 15-20s. Focus on ankle corrections.
Switch legs.

injuryNotes.knee: BOSU increases joint demand. Knee severity ≤2. Very challenging for unstable knee.

purpose: Advanced proprioception — unstable surface + single leg = maximum ankle/hip challenge.
whyForYou: Simulates the instability of surfing and uneven terrain. Requires excellent joint stability.
proTip: Start on dome side (easier). Progress to flat side up (much harder).
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Balance Progression

name: Single-Leg Hop to Stabilization

prerequisites.notes: Phase 2+. Knee severity ≤1 (BLOCKED for John currently). Plyometric readiness required.

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: Stick 5 landings each leg with zero knee valgus

steps: Stand on one leg. Small hop forward.
LAND softly on same leg. STICK the landing — hold 3s.
Knee must track perfectly — zero valgus.
If knee caves at all, this exercise is too advanced.

injuryNotes.knee: BLOCKED for you currently (severity 2 > gate of 1). This is a PLYOMETRIC — requires knee severity ≤1 and plyometric readiness. Future goal after full knee recovery.

purpose: Dynamic balance with landing — tests and trains landing mechanics under real conditions.
whyForYou: Future goal. Currently blocked by knee severity. Shows full athletic recovery when accessible.
proTip: Start with tiny hops — literally 2 inches. Build height/distance over weeks.
source: NSCA, ACL return-to-sport protocols

name: Tandem Stance Walk (Heel-to-Toe)
steps: Stand near a wall for safety.
Walk in a straight line — heel of front foot touches toe of back foot.
Look forward, not down. Arms at sides.
20 steps. Turn. 20 steps back.
formCues: ✅ Arms at sides (not out for balance)

injuryNotes.knee: Narrow base challenges knee stability. Knee severity ≤3.

purpose: Dynamic balance in a narrow base of support — simulates real-world gait challenges.
whyForYou: Trains balance during walking — directly relevant to hiking trails and uneven surfaces.
proTip: Close your eyes for the ultimate challenge (near a wall). This is also a sobriety field test — pass it every time.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Balance Assessment

name: Squat to Stand

prerequisites.notes: BW squat and hamstring flexibility required

steps: Stand with feet hip-width. Fold forward — hands touch floor.
Grab toes. Drop hips into deep squat (as much as comfortable). Chest up.
Release toes. Stand up through squat position.
Fold forward again. Repeat the flow.

injuryNotes.lower_back: Involves flexion — back severity ≤3. Don't force the toe touch. Go to comfortable range.
injuryNotes.knee: Squat depth limited by comfort. Knee severity ≤3.

purpose: Combined hip hinge + squat mobility — opens hamstrings, hips, and ankles in one flow.
whyForYou: Efficient mobility drill combining your tightest areas into one flowing movement.
proTip: Hold the bottom squat position for 3 breaths — that's where the magic happens.
source: FMS (Functional Movement Systems), Gray Cook

name: Inchworm

prerequisites.notes: Plank ability required for the walk-out portion

progressionChain.unlockCriteria: 6 reps smooth with plank position maintained

steps: Stand. Fold forward, hands to floor (bend knees if needed).
Walk hands out to plank position. Hold 1-2s.
Walk hands BACK to feet. Stand up.
Add a push-up at plank position for progression.
formCues: ✅ Keep legs as straight as comfortable

injuryNotes.lower_back: Walk-out creates extension demand. Back severity ≤3. If back sags, reduce range.
injuryNotes.knee: No impact — legs stay relatively straight.
injuryNotes.shoulder: Plank position loads shoulders. Shoulder severity ≤3. If bothered, stop at walk-out without plank hold.

purpose: Full-body dynamic warm-up — hamstring flexibility, core stability, and shoulder engagement in one flow.
whyForYou: Combines hamstring stretch with core work — efficient warm-up hitting two of your key areas.
proTip: Add a push-up at the plank position for a more complete warm-up drill.
source: NASM CPT 7th Edition — Dynamic Warm-Up
